executiveSummary:
**EXECUTIVE SUMMARY: MEDICAID PROGRAM STATUS BRIEFING**

National Medicaid enrollment totaled 75.3 million beneficiaries as of January 2026, with widespread enrollment declines reflecting the ongoing post-pandemic eligibility redeterminations. Nine of the ten largest states experienced year-over-year enrollment decreases, ranging from modest declines in New York (-1.8%) to significant reductions in Michigan (-7.6%). California and Illinois led with 6.2% decreases, while only North Carolina among major states posted growth (+0.5%). Total program expenditures reached $215.9 billion in fiscal year 2024, with a national per-enrollee cost of $2,869. Notable spending variations emerged across states, with Pennsylvania ($4,377 per enrollee) and Massachusetts ($4,201) significantly exceeding the national average, while Florida maintained the lowest per-capita costs at $2,031.

Texas, with 4.0 million enrollees and a 3.1% year-over-year decline, demonstrates mixed performance relative to peer states. The state's per-enrollee spending of $2,988 falls just above the national average and compares favorably to similar large states like Ohio ($3,561) and Pennsylvania ($4,377), though it exceeds more efficient programs in North Carolina ($2,616) and Illinois ($2,715). However, Texas quality performance lags significantly, ranking 30th nationally with a score of 62 out of 100, placing it in the moderate tier but well below top-performing states like Massachusetts (81) and Minnesota (80). The state's managed care penetration rate of 93.3% substantially exceeds the national average of 83.2%, suggesting strong infrastructure for care coordination despite quality measurement challenges.

Key trends warranting attention include the continued enrollment normalization following pandemic-era continuous coverage policies, persistent quality performance gaps in Southern and Southwestern states, and emerging federal regulatory activity around home and community-based services quality measures for 2028. States should prepare for additional CMS oversight initiatives, as evidenced by active HHS OIG work plans and new data collection requirements, while leveraging high managed care penetration rates to drive quality improvements and cost containment strategies.
texasSpotlight:
## Texas Medicaid Program Performance Analysis

Texas operates the nation's third-largest Medicaid program with 4.03 million enrollees, representing a substantial 5.4% of national enrollment despite serving only 8.8% of the U.S. population. The state demonstrates fiscal discipline with per-enrollee spending of $2,988—4.2% above the national average of $2,869 but significantly below peer states like Pennsylvania ($4,377) and Ohio ($3,561). This cost efficiency becomes more notable when considering Texas has not expanded Medicaid under the ACA, unlike expansion states that typically serve higher-cost adult populations. The state's enrollment declined 3.1% year-over-year, closely tracking the national trend as states complete post-pandemic eligibility redeterminations, with the decrease slightly better than large peer states like Illinois (-6.2%) and Michigan (-7.6%). Texas's robust managed care infrastructure covers 93.3% of enrollees, well above the 83.2% national average, indicating strong systems for care coordination and cost management.

However, Texas faces significant quality challenges that warrant immediate attention. The state ranks 30th nationally with a quality score of 62/100, placing it in the "moderate" tier but below the national average of 65.6. This performance is particularly concerning when compared to southern peers like North Carolina, which achieved notable improvements, and represents a critical area where Texas lags behind its operational efficiency. The state's quality performance mirrors patterns seen in other non-expansion southern states, suggesting systemic challenges in care access and outcomes that may stem from coverage gaps and provider network constraints. Moving forward, policymakers should monitor whether Texas's strong managed care penetration can be leveraged to drive quality improvements, particularly as federal quality reporting requirements intensify and value-based payment models expand. The state's large scale and managed care sophistication position it well to implement quality initiatives, but sustained focus and investment will be essential to move from moderate to high performance tiers.

alerts.title: Pennsylvania Per-Enrollee Spending 53% Above National Average
alerts.description: Pennsylvania's per-enrollee spending of $4,377 is significantly higher than the national average of $2,869, indicating potential cost control issues or unique population needs.

alerts.title: Five States Show Persistently Poor Quality Performance
alerts.description: Arkansas, South Carolina, Alabama, Oklahoma, and Mississippi all scored below 55 on quality measures, with Mississippi at critically low 48/100.

alerts.title: Florida Shows Low Per-Enrollee Spending Despite Large Program
alerts.description: Florida's per-enrollee spending of $2,031 is 29% below the national average despite having the 4th largest Medicaid enrollment, suggesting efficient operations or potential underfunding.

alerts.title: Texas Combines Below-Average Spending with Moderate Quality
alerts.description: Texas achieves moderate quality scores (62/100, rank #30) while maintaining per-enrollee spending ($2,988) close to the national average, with high managed care penetration at 93.3%.

alerts.title: Massachusetts Achieves Top Quality Despite High Spending
alerts.description: Massachusetts leads in quality scores (81/100) but has the highest per-enrollee spending at $4,201, demonstrating potential correlation between investment and outcomes.

executiveInsights.title: Texas quality score significantly below national average at 62/100
executiveInsights.summary: Texas ranks #30 out of 50 states with a quality score of 62/100, well below the national average of 65.6/100. This places Texas in the moderate tier, dangerously close to the low performer threshold of <60.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Quality improvement is likely a top priority for HHSC leadership, creating immediate opportunities for Accenture to position quality enhancement services and demonstrate value through measurable outcomes.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Schedule immediate meeting with HHSC Quality team to discuss improvement initiatives

executiveInsights.title: Texas per-enrollee costs 4% above national average at $2,988
executiveInsights.summary: Texas spends $2,988 per enrollee compared to the national average of $2,868.64, representing a $119 premium per member. With 4M+ enrollees, this translates to approximately $480M in excess costs annually.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Cost efficiency gaps this large signal potential for significant savings initiatives that Accenture could lead, while also indicating budget pressure that may drive procurement decisions.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Prepare cost optimization analysis comparing TX to efficient peer states like IL and NC

executiveInsights.title: Texas enrollment declining at 3.1% YoY with 4M+ members affected
executiveInsights.summary: Texas Medicaid enrollment dropped 3.1% year-over-year, affecting over 4 million enrollees. This decline rate is better than several large states like California (-6.2%) and Michigan (-7.6%).
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Enrollment changes directly impact contract value and operational requirements, requiring immediate assessment of how declining membership affects our service delivery and revenue projections.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review contract terms for enrollment-based adjustments and update revenue forecasts

executiveInsights.title: New 2028 HCBS quality measures announced via Federal Register
executiveInsights.summary: CMS published new Medicaid Home and Community-Based Services Quality Measure Set for 2028, indicating upcoming regulatory changes. This appears in recent Federal Register filings from April 2026.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: New quality measures will require system updates and process changes, creating opportunity for Accenture to support HHSC's compliance preparation and potentially improve Texas's current quality ranking.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag to HHSC compliance team and assess implementation timeline requirements

executiveInsights.title: Texas managed care penetration at 93.3% exceeds national average of 83.2%
executiveInsights.summary: Texas has 93.3% managed care penetration with 3.86M enrollees in managed care, significantly above the national average of 83.2%. This represents strong managed care infrastructure utilization.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: High managed care penetration indicates sophisticated operational requirements and potential for advanced analytics and care management solutions that align with Accenture's capabilities.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review managed care optimization opportunities and advanced analytics applications

riskOpportunity.label: Enrollment decline management
riskOpportunity.description: Texas Medicaid enrollment dropped 3.1% YoY, creating system optimization needs and potential contract adjustments.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Propose enrollment stabilization and system rightsizing services to HHSC.

riskOpportunity.label: Quality score improvement
riskOpportunity.description: Texas ranks #30 nationally with 62/100 quality score, below the 65.6 national average.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop comprehensive quality improvement program targeting top-performing state benchmarks.

riskOpportunity.label: Managed care recompete
riskOpportunity.description: High 93.3% managed care penetration creates significant procurement risk and opportunity for system updates.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Monitor HHSC procurement portal closely and prepare comprehensive managed care proposal.

riskOpportunity.label: OIG oversight exposure
riskOpportunity.description: Active HHS OIG Medicaid portfolio oversight increases compliance scrutiny and remediation demand.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Strengthen compliance monitoring capabilities and offer proactive audit readiness services.

riskOpportunity.label: HCBS quality measures
riskOpportunity.description: New 2028 Medicaid Home and Community-Based Services quality measure requirements create implementation needs.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Position Accenture as the lead implementer for HCBS quality measurement systems in Texas.

riskOpportunity.label: Per-enrollee cost efficiency
riskOpportunity.description: Texas spends $2,988 per enrollee versus $2,869 national average, indicating potential efficiency gaps.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Propose cost optimization analytics and management services to achieve national benchmark performance.

riskOpportunity.label: Competitive market pressure
riskOpportunity.description: Multiple active procurement opportunities and competitor positioning threaten market share retention.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Accelerate relationship building with key HHSC decision makers and differentiate service offerings.
